data.title: AST SpaceMobile schedules late February for BlueBird 7 launch
data.description:
Shares of AST SpaceMobile Inc. (ASTS) are up 4.5% in early open market trading on Thursday after the company announced that it has scheduled the BlueBird 7 mission launch for late February.

The satellites will be launched from Launch Complex 36 at Cape Canaveral Space Force Station on Blue Origin’s (BORGN) New Glenn launch vehicle. Future missions on New Glenn are expected to deliver up to 8 next-generation BlueBirds per flight.

BlueBird 7, which is similar to BlueBird 6, is the second satellite in AST SpaceMobile’s next-generation campaign. It measures nearly 2,400 square feet and features the "largest commercial communications array in low Earth orbit," 3.5 times larger than BlueBirds 1-5, the company said.

data.title: Northern Trust Q4 earnings, revenue beats helped by NII, servicing fee growth
data.description:
Northern Trust (NTRS) climbed 6.3% in Thursday morning trading after delivering a double beat in Q4, helped by better-than-expected net interest income growth and strong asset servicing income.

Q4 GAAP EPS of $2.42, vs. $2.35 consensus, rose from $2.29 in Q3 and $2.26 in Q4 2024. The current quarter included a pretax expense of $19.2M related to Visa (V) class B swaps, a $58.8M pretax expense for severance-related charges, and a $9.5M pretax release of FDIC special assessment reserve.

Total revenue (fully taxable equivalent) of $2.14B, beating the average analyst estimate of $2.06B, increased from $2.03B in the previous quarter and $1.97B in the year-ago period.

"Mid-single-digit trust fee growth, double-digit net interest income, and disciplined expense management drove positive operating leverage of four points in the fourth quarter and more than two points for the full year, both excluding notables," said Chairman and CEO Michael O'Grady.

Net interest income ((FTE)) of $654.3M, exceeding the Visible Alpha consensus of $601.2M, grew from $596.3M in Q3 and $574.3M in the year-ago Q4. Net interest margin ((FTE)) improved to 1.81% from 1.70% in Q3 and 1.71% in Q4 2024.

Client assets under custody/administration hit $18.7T, up 3% Q/Q and 11% Y/Y.

Trust, investment, and other servicing fees totaled $1.31B, up 3% Q/Q and 7% Y/Y.

Asset servicing income before taxes ((FTE)) of $325.8M rose 11% Q/Q and 23% Y/Y, while wealth management income before taxes ((FTE)) was essentially flat Q/Q and slipped 3% Y/Y.

Total average deposits of $119.8B gained 3% Q/Q and 6% Y/Y. Average total loans of $40.6B fell 2% Q/Q and 1% Y/Y.

Q4 noninterest expense of $1.50B grew 5% Q/Q and 9% Y/Y, with compensation expense of $686.8M increasing 10% from the prior quarter and 15% from a year ago.

data.title: Norwood Financial outlines integration and operational efficiency initiatives following Presence Bank acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Norwood Financial Corp. (NWFL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO James Donnelly highlighted the successful closure of the Presence Bank acquisition, noting, "We expanded our net interest spread by 62 basis points, increasing net interest income, 62% compared with the fourth quarter of 2024. Net income and earnings per share more than doubled on an adjusted basis, and we improved returns on both average assets and tangible equity." Donnelly emphasized that the acquisition, which closed on January 5, increased the asset base by 20% and added four branches in Southeast and South Central Pennsylvania.
  • The CEO outlined four strategic priorities for 2026: the integration of Presence Bank, leveraging advanced AI tools for operational efficiency, strengthening the leadership team, and maintaining a focus on shareholder value. Donnelly stated, "Presence Bank has implemented advanced AI tools in their commercial systems, which we are adopting as part of our integration. We are using AI to supplement and enhance the work of our talented credit officers in drafted credit narratives, summarizing financials and confirm required documentation. This will allow us to underwrite deals more quickly and to do more deals with our existing team."
  • New appointments include Janak Amin as Chief Operating Officer, Larry Witt as Chief Information Officer, and Doug Byers as Market Executive and Head of Treasury Management. Joseph Carroll and Spencer Andress also joined the Board of Directors.
  • CFO John McCaffery stated, "For the fourth quarter, we once again demonstrated our ability to improve financial results, continuing the trend that began with our balance sheet repositioning back in December 2024. Our net interest income increased by $0.5 million on a linked-quarter basis, while the margin itself did drop 3 basis points, this was due to loan growth in the quarter as well as some outflow -- seasonal outflow of municipal deposits on a temporary basis."

Outlook

  • Management outlined integration of Presence Bank and deployment of advanced AI tools as operational priorities for 2026, aiming to increase efficiency and improve customer experience. The CEO stated, "We will manage our deposits and assets to maintain our strong financial position, ensuring that we are positioned to continue serving our communities for years to come."
  • Strategic focus remains on asset growth through deposits, investment decisions, and M&A, combined with a capital allocation framework that sustains a growing dividend.
  • Management expressed optimism for 2026, citing momentum from 2025 and a strengthened team.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income increased by $0.5 million on a linked-quarter basis. The net interest margin declined by 3 basis points, attributed to loan growth and temporary outflows of municipal deposits.
  • Quarterly expenses were up 1.5% year-over-year, and increased 5% on a linked-quarter basis, due to lower loan volumes, vesting of restricted stock for retiring employees, and higher incentive accruals.
  • The quarter included $520,000 in merger charges. Noninterest income for the year increased, primarily from fees on loans and deposit products.
  • Credit metrics improved year-over-year, with nonperforming loans as a percentage of total loans decreasing and reserves to nonperforming assets increasing.

Q&A

  • NOT_APPLICABLE. The transcript indicates, "I am not showing any questions at this time." No analyst questions or management responses were recorded.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: No questions were posed, so no sentiment can be gauged from the analyst side this quarter.
  • Management: The tone was positive and confident during prepared remarks, with CEO Donnelly stating, "I am pleased with our performance in 2025 and proud of what we were able to accomplish." The management team projected optimism regarding integration efforts and future growth.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s confidence increased, shifting from a focus on completing regulatory steps for the Presence Bank acquisition to celebrating its closure and planning integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The fourth quarter saw the completion of the Presence Bank acquisition, a significant development from the prior quarter where regulatory approvals were pending.
  • Strategic priorities have shifted from preparation and regulatory filings to active integration, operational enhancement via AI, and leadership expansion.
  • Management tone has moved from cautious optimism regarding M&A and growth to celebratory and forward-looking, reflecting confidence in the expanded team and asset base.
  • No analyst questions this quarter, contrasting with a more interactive Q3 session that included inquiries about deposit costs, NIM outlook, and M&A timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged merger-related expenses and seasonal municipal deposit outflows impacting short-term results.
  • The integration of Presence Bank presents operational and cultural alignment challenges, but management indicated a measured approach to limit disruptions.
  • No analyst concerns were raised due to a lack of Q&A.

Final Takeaway

Norwood Financial Corp. closed 2025 with strong momentum, highlighted by the strategic acquisition of Presence Bank, which expanded its geographic footprint and talent base. Management is focused on integrating advanced operational technologies and leveraging new leadership to drive efficiency and growth in 2026. The company’s financial results benefited from portfolio repositioning and loan growth, while expense management and credit quality remained solid. The leadership team remains confident about future prospects, emphasizing continued value creation for shareholders and a commitment to robust financial performance following a transformative year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Fund is right at its 200-day MA
data.description:
The United States Natural Gas Fund, LP ETF’s (UNG) current price has been balancing around its 200-day moving average – $14.36 – on Thursday, with a price day range of $14.17 and $14.35.

 

 

 

The U.S. gas fund (UNG) is up 39.8% this week and up 16.8% year-to-date so far, although its Seeking Alpha momentum grade is a D-.

Also, vs. the S&P 500 (SP500), the fund is down 23.4% from a year ago, although it went up 24.1% this month so far in price return.

Its RSI reading is at 62.21, getting close to overbought territory.

Overall, U.S. natural gas (UNG) has extended its rally driven by freezing temperatures this winter, surging more than 50% in two days.

"Volatility will stay high, however, and any weather model warming into mid-February could allow a near-term price spike to eventually soften," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics said in a note.

Natural Gas ETFs: (UNG), (BOIL), and (UNL).

data.title: Biggest stock movers Thursday: DDOG, BABA, ABT, and more
data.description:
Stock futures inched higher Thursday as easing geopolitical fears drove a broad market rally, with traders digesting a softer-than-expected inflation reading alongside resilient labor data.

Biggest stock gainers

  • Alibaba (BABA) +7% - Shares gained following a report that the company is preparing its chip unit, T-Head, for a potential IPO as it looks to tap strong investor demand for Nvidia rivals in the AI accelerator market. Alibaba is restructuring T-Head into a standalone business with partial employee ownership, a first step before exploring an eventual listing, though timing and valuation remain uncertain. Alibaba has long invested in chip design to support its cloud and AI ambitions, and positioning T-Head for a future IPO underscores its push to reduce reliance on external suppliers and compete more aggressively in AI hardware.
  • Datadog (DDOG) +6% - Shares rose after Stifel upgraded the stock to Buy from Hold, citing confidence that the company will outperform expectations as core growth accelerates. Analyst Brad Reback said management should be able to guide 2026 revenue to roughly $4.1B, adding that Datadog’s valuation looks appealing given its strengthening fundamentals. Stifel set a $160 price target, implying about 30% upside from the prior close.
  • Karman Holdings (KRMN) +5% - Shares surged after the company raised its FY2025 outlook and issued a strong FY2026 forecast, driven by the Seemann and MSC acquisitions that expand its reach into high-priority U.S. Navy programs. Karman now expects FY2025 revenue of $470M–$471M, ahead of the consensus of $464.01M, with adjusted EBITDA of $144.5M–$144.9M, while FY2026 guidance also came in well above expectations with revenue of $700M–$715M (consensus $577.65M) and adjusted EBITDA of $205M–$215M. Management highlighted that the acquisitions will be immediately accretive across revenue, EBITDA, EPS, and cash flow, reinforcing the company’s position among high-margin defense technology peers.

Biggest stock losers

  • Abbott (ABT) -8% - Shares plunged after Q4 revenue came in light, with net sales of $11.5B rising ~4% Y/Y but falling short of the $11.8B estimate as Nutrition revenue dropped ~9% amid softer volumes and planned pricing actions. Nutrition contributed $1.9B vs. the $2.2B analysts projected, offsetting strong performances in MedTech and Pharma, which grew ~12% and ~9% Y/Y, respectively, to $5.7B and $1.4B. Diagnostics declined ~3% to $2.5B, missing consensus by about $80M as COVID-testing revenue halved to $89M. While CEO Robert Ford highlighted margin expansion, a productive pipeline, and strategic progress in 2025, guidance disappointed: 2026 organic sales growth of 6.5%–7.5% landed slightly below expectations at the midpoint, and Q1 adjusted EPS guidance of $1.12–$1.18 missed the $1.20 Street view, though full-year EPS of $5.55–$5.80 aligned with forecasts.
  • T1 Energy (TE) -4% - Shares slipped after short seller Culper Research disclosed a bearish position in the stock. The company also filed a prospectus supplement for the potential resale of 14.3M shares and a warrant for 7M additional shares held by existing investors Trina Solar and Stellar Hann Investment. The filing allows those security holders to resell previously issued shares or shares tied to the warrant, though it doesn’t require them to sell or generate any proceeds for the company.
  • Axogen (AXGN) -2% - Shares fell after announcing plans to raise $85M through an underwritten public offering of common stock, with underwriters receiving a 30-day option to buy an additional $12.75M in shares. All shares will be sold by the company, which intends to use the proceeds to repay and terminate its Oberland Capital term loan and fund working capital, capex, and other general corporate needs.

data.title: Mobileye falls after 2026 revenue outlook seen below estimates
data.description:
Shares of Mobileye Global (MBLY) fell about 5% on Thursday after the company's full year 2026 revenue guidance came below analysts' expectations.

Outlook

The Israeli company — which develops autonomous driving technology and advanced driver assistance systems — expects full year 2026 revenue to be between $1.9B and $1.98B (midpoint at $1.94B) versus a consensus revenue estimate of $2B.

The company said full-year 2026 financial guidance implies flat to 5% year-over-year revenue growth, including about 19% year-over-year revenue growth expected in the first quarter of 2026.

Mobileye said it expects 2026 adjusted operating income in the range of $170M to $220M.

Q4 Metrics

The company's fourth quarter 2025 revenue declined 9% year over year to $446M but surpassed analysts expectations. Meanwhile, adjusted EPS of $0.06 fell 58% year over year but was in-line with estimates.

Mobileye said the revenue declined mainly due to an 11% reduction in EyeQ SoC volumes. This was primarily related to a balancing of supply and demand that resulted in tighter-than-normal inventory levels at its Tier 1 customers exiting 2025, according to the company.

"I am proud of the Mobileye team for delivering 2025 results that were well above our initial expectations. We enter 2026 with strong momentum and a cash-generative business that enables us to fund continued investment in advanced product execution across our portfolio," said Mobileye's President and CEO Amnon Shashua.

Intel (INTC) owns a stake in Mobileye.

Analysts Commentary

Analysts led by Andrew Percoco at Morgan Stanley said the stock is trading down, likely driven by a disappointing guide despite the in-line quarter. The analysts added that at the midpoint of 2026 guidance, the implied EBIT margin is 5 percentage points below expectation (about 10.1% versus 15.2% consensus and 14.4% Morgan Stanley estimates).

"MBLY posted an inline 4Q, yet the focus today will be on '26 guidance which was below expectations," said Barclays' analysts led by Dan Levy.

However, the analysts noted that the guidance was directionally in line with prior commentary for EyeQ volume growth to outpace core customers by 5 points (with core customer volume down 2%).

The analysts added that the larger surprise was within EBIT guidance of $170M to $220M, below consensus of $300M and Barclays estimate of $259M. 

"We believe the higher opex is likely best explained by a stronger Israel Shekel (opex largely in Israel Shekel), with the Mentee acquisition also adding some opex.. these factors more than offset the benefit from MBLY's recent headcount reduction," said Levy and his team.

"While we still see an attractive opportunity ahead for MBLY with the stock at a depressed level, the 4Q result reflected a rightsizing of expectations," the analysts added.

Analysts Joseph Spak and his team at UBS said the fourth quarter results were fine, but 2026 guidance looks light with 2026 revenue (at midpoint) guided 3% below consensus and adjusted operating income 36% below. 

The analysts added that the guidance does include Mentee opex that they believe adds $30M to $40M in opex.

"This was in our forecast, but we don't believe is in consensus so adjusting for this the guide would have been ~24% below consensus. We believe the other main headwind to Ol [operating income] is the appreciation of the Israeli Shekel which at spot looks to be ~9% higher y/y," said Spak and his team.

Update: The story was updated with stock movement and analysts commentary.

data.title: Moderna extends YTD rally to hit 52-week high
data.description:
Moderna (MRNA) shares reached a new 52-week high on Thursday as the COVID-19 vaccine maker extended its recent gains to record a nearly 84% jump for this year alone after multiple years of consecutive declines.

The Cambridge, Massachusetts-based biotech last recorded an annual rally in 2021 when its shares more than doubled on the back of how its COVID-19 vaccine, Spikevax, which was the second to receive U.S. authorization, contributed to fighting the pandemic.

However, the stock recorded back-to-back annual losses in the subsequent years, and MRNA’s market capitalization plummeted from more than $180B in 2021 to roughly $12B by the end of 2025 as the pandemic’s impact on public health waned.

The upsurge comes as the company pivots its portfolio to other disease areas. Moderna (MRNA) was the third to receive U.S. authorization for a vaccine targeting respiratory syncytial virus ((RSV)) in 2025 when the FDA approved its RSV shot mRESVIA .

This week, the company and its partner Merck (MRK) posted encouraging long-term efficacy and safety data from a mid-stage trial for their jointly developed messenger RNA-based cancer vaccine, mRNA-4157, in certain patients with melanoma.

data.title: Intel shows mixed earnings revision, momentum metrics point towards strength
data.description:
Intel has rallied about 11% this week heading into its fourth quarter earnings release on Thursday.

Seeking Alpha's quarterly earnings revisions page paints a mixed picture. Analyst updates show a split community, with revisions leaning slightly negative on the critical top line despite the stock's surge, as bulls and bears offset each other without clear momentum toward upgrades. 

Wall Street expects the chipmaker to post EPS of $0.08 on revenue of $13.39 billion, implying a fall of 6% during the quarter.

Further down, Wall Street consensus implies just 7% year‑ahead revenue growth, modest at best given the AI hype around Intel's accelerators and foundry buildout, suggesting much of the narrative may already be in the price.

Seeking Alpha's Quant Ratings recommends Intel as a Hold with a score of 3.49 out of 5.

Heading into earnings season, J.P. Morgan said many semiconductor firms appear to have "considerable upside" to the AI accelerator total addressable market.

Momentum metrics on Seeking Alpha support the short‑term strength but lack conviction on sustainability, setting up Intel for a high‑stakes test where a beat could validate the rally and force estimate chasing, while any revenue shortfall or cautious guidance risks a swift unwind.

data.title: BNP Paribas eyes stronger demand for analog; upgrades Texas Instruments, Onsemi, Microchip
data.description:
BNP Paribas has identified a broad-based restocking cycle for semiconductors across industrial end markets, which it believes should provide upside for analog products.

This should drive above-average seasonal growth for the next few quarters, prompting the global financial firm to upgrade several companies, including Texas Instruments (TXN), Onsemi (ON) and Microchip Technology (MCHP). 

Texas Instruments and Onsemi were both upgraded to Neutral from Underperform. BNP also assigned them new price targets of $190 and $62, respectively.

Microchip was upgraded to Outperform from Neutral and assigned a $90 price target.

"Our feedback from CES meetings and recent industry conversations is that a restocking cycle is underway in Industrial semis," said BNP Paribas analyst David O'Connor. "Nine quarters post the Q3'23 analog peak and painful inventory correction, inventories are too low and need to be restocked. A restocking cycle typically takes a few quarters to complete and is likely to drive above seasonal growth."

"This is also a leading indicator of industrial customers feeling better about H2 demand, which bodes well for the broader industrial space," he added. "We believe this restocking is broad-based across technologies and geographies."

About 50% of Microchip's sales stem from the industrial sector, while industrial accounts for 30% of Onsemi's revenue and 34% for Texas Instruments.  

However, O'Connor pointed out that auto semi demand remains low but could see a recovery in 2027. The auto end market represents nearly half of Onsemi's revenue.

"We expect the semi market to grow 18% y/y in 2026, (9 pts from memory), with analog semis growing 10% y/y driven by Industrial restocking," O'Connor noted. "Analog Revenues/EPS remain 25-56% below previous peaks on average, and investors are still underweight. With the next stage of the cyclical recovery now underway, we like the setup and are turning more positive on Analog."
